segments.text: Allergies, and Autoimmunity
Types of Hypersensitivity

Ambrosia artemissiifolia                                                        pollen
Common or short ragweed
Ambrosia trifida
Giant ragweed
-15-25 uM diameter
-Pectate Lyase is the major cause of hay fever, it is “Antigen E”
Ragweed causes “Hay Fever”
Ragweed is native to North America, but is invasive
Type I Hypersensitivity

Type I Hypersensitivity
Initial exposure to
Antigen
IgE

Allergic Reaction
IgE  binds Fc receptors on mast cells and basophils

Mast Cells release:
TNFa-pro-inflammatory cytokine
Histamine-increases permeability of capillaries for leukocytes

Loratadine (Claritin) blocks one type of histamine receptor (the H1 receptor) and thus prevents activation of cells with H1 receptors by histamine. Unlike some antihistamines, loratadine does not enter the brain from the blood and, therefore, does not cause drowsiness when taken at recommended doses.
“Wheel and Flare” reaction (swelling and reddening)

Intracellular pathogens
Extracellular pathogens
Suppresses immune response
Secretes Il-17
There are many types of Helper T-cells

Immunotherapy induced desensitization
Note: IgG
Fails to induce degranulation
TH1 cells then activate IgG secretion

Hygiene Hypothesis
The lack of early childhood exposure to antigens, increases susceptibility to allergic diseases by suppressing the natural development of some components of the immune system

urushiol
Poison Ivy is a Type IV hypersensitivity

Delayed-type Hypersensitivity
No Antibodies
Langerhans cells are a type of dendritic cell found in the epidermis

Negative Selection
“Indisputably, the primary cause of autoimmune disease is the escape of self reactive  lymphocytes B-cells, T-cells, or both from negative selection.  Surprisingly little is known about the reasons for this escape.

Types of Hypersensitivity
Type 1 Diabetes

Type I diabetes is an autoimmune disorder
In type I diabetes the beta cells that make insulin, in the pancreas are destroyed.
In type II diabetes the cells of the body resist the action of insulin
Symptoms:
Increased thirst
Frequent urination
Increased hunger
Unintended weight loss
Fatigue
Blurred vision
Slow-healing sores
Pancreas anatomy
Islet of Langerhans
Beta cells produce insulin

Insulin
Proinsulin
Insulin
Insulin stimulates uptake of glucose from bloodstream

nonobese diabetic  mouse (NOD)
Spontaneously develops a form of diabetes that resembles human T1D
Cytotoxic T cell infiltration of islet
Autoantibodies found in T1D
Types of Hypersensitivity

Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
Self antigens:
DNA
Nuclear proteins
RBC
Platelet membranes
Signs and symptoms:
Fever
Rash
Weakness
Arthritis
Kidney dysfunction
Occlusion of small blood vessels
Mouse models of Lupus

Important Terms/Concepts
Mast cell characteristics, activation and response
Hay fever as an example of an IgE hypersensitivity
E antigen is pectate lyase
Hygiene hypothesis
Poison Ivy as an example of a delayed type hypersensitivity
Type I diabetes as an example of an autoimmune disorder
Beta cells, insulin, GLUT
Lupus as an example of a systemic autoimmune disorder
Females are more susceptible to autoimmune disorders

IgM
first class of antibody found on B cell membrane
secreted as a pentamer
contains J chain
good at agglutination
important secondary role (complement) in secreted antibodies
IgD
No biological effector function has been identified.
IgG
secondary response
most abundant, making up 80% of serum immunoglobulin
four subclasses
crosses placenta
IgE
binds to Fc receptors on basophils and mast cells, stimulating degranulation of histamine
“E” comes from antigen E of ragweed
IgA
predominant class of secreted antibody
more of this secreted than all other antibodies combined
is trancytosed, like IgM

SYSTEMIC: AFFECTS MANY ORGANS
Typical systemic autoimmune diseases are rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, scleroderma, and dermatomyositis.
ORGAN-SPECIFIC: AFFECTS ONE MAIN ORGAN
Organ-specific autoimmune diseases are those where a particular organ or tissue is preferentially targeted by the patient's immune system. For example, the thyroid gland in patients with Graves disease, the beta cells of the endocrine pancreas in patients with type 1 diabetes, or the skin in patients with vitiligo.
Examples of Autoimmune disorders

Cytokines direct naïve CD4+ cells into either a TH1 or TH2
-Promotes TC growth
-activates macrophages
-Hypersensitivity
-Activates B-cell production of antibodies
-very effectively activates B-cell production of antibodies

Lymphocytes bound to HEVs
High Endothelial Venules (HEVs) are sites of lymphocyte extravasation (lymph node)

Mast cells
-key players in the initiation of inflammation
-are found in all vascularized tissues.
-Their cytoplasm is loaded with granules containing mediators of inflammation, histamine is a key player
-Their surface is coated with a variety of receptors which, when engaged by the appropriate ligand, trigger exocytosis of the granules.
-Toll-like receptors trigger exocytosis when they interact with Pathogen Associated Molecular Patterns (PAMPs) like
The lipopolysaccharide  LPS or “endotoxin” of Gram negative bacteria (TLR-4)
The peptidoglycan of Gram-positive bacteria (TLR-2)
-Their receptors for complement fragments trigger exocytosis when they bind C3a and C5a or bacteria coated with C3d
IgE  binds Fc receptors on mast cells and basophils

Allergic Contact Dermititis
TRPA1 is an ion channel found in neurons (cations Na, Ca)
Stimulated by the neurotransmitter Substance P
Oxazolone is used to induce dermititis
